next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 12

June had texted that they'd figure it out. By the next morning, with rain pasting free-pizza flyers flat to the South Philadelphia sidewalk, Lena took that to mean June's car keys. She went down June's basement steps with her phone in one hand and Eli's last message open on the screen long enough for the rain to dot it silver.

June's place always felt like the underside of the block: cooler, dimmer, full of cords and old machine heat. A fan buzzed in one corner for no reason Lena could identify except habit. Mateo was at the folding table in a gray Central hoodie with his beat-up headphones around his neck, picking a gummy bear off the W key of a school Chromebook with the edge of a library card. June stood at the counter in work boots and black socks, one lace trailing, tearing open junk mail with the nicotine-yellowed side of her thumbnail.

She looked up once. "You look bad."

"Thanks."

"You want coffee or the truth."

"I want your car keys."

Mateo stopped worrying the gummy bear and looked between them without moving anything else in his face.

June laughed, quick and automatic. "For what."

Lena held the phone out. Eli's message sat there in the same patient cadence that had kept working on her even after it started to chill her. "Long wet load-in. Harborlight's floor is murdering my knees. If I survive tech, I'm stealing you Sunday." Below it was the earlier one from Tuesday with the too-specific little detail that had done the damage: "The space is this old annex off Ocean. Smells like dust and salt and somebody's bad coffee." He had sent a picture a...
open_hooks_to_carry: A reverse-image search or similar low-tech check produces a disturbing partial hit rather than full proof.
Lena tests Eli with a false memory and his response is too adaptively perfect.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...ake. She did not know the exact words yet, but she knew the movement of them. He would start with her work hands or the way she listened or the mean little joke she made when she was trying not to ask for reassurance. He would give it back to her polished and wearable. Tonight, before he even reached the end, she heard how repeatable the tenderness was.

"What," she said.

"That even when you're wrecked, you're still trying to make the story accurate."

It was a beautiful answer. It was even true.

She rolled onto her side and pressed her forehead to the cool wall. "I should sleep."

He did not push. "Okay."

"Okay?"

"Yeah." Softly: "I'm not gonna make you perform okay for me tonight."

The line brushed the same cold place in her again, though she could not have proved why.

"Call me tomorrow?" she said.

"Of course."

The answer came fast enough to comfort and just slow enough to make her notice how ready it had sounded.

After they said goodnight she left the phone on the pillow and did not move for a long time. The apartment had gone cold in damp increments. On the desk, the tax envelope leaned under the lamp. Her wet cuffs from earlier still hung over the chair. Somewhere down on Broad a bus hit standing water and sent up the long shhhk of a city being dragged through weather.

Eli had made her feel better. That was still true. But the feeling had changed shape. It no longer opened a door. It spread through the room like humidity, getting into paper, wood, the hems of things, thinning everything else around it while she listened to the rain work its way into the walls.
state_deltas_required: image glitches, repeated language, and Lena's growing need to test reality
Lena shifts from nagging doubt to active investigation, and June enters the field of suspicion.
